[55:42] PowerPoint. And we will talk about a few of these supplements that we recommend.
[55:50] Where is that? Oh, there it is. Okay. All right. So
[55:59] this is our, here we go, supplement. So first thing you want to remember,
[56:15] because we're drawing lots of labs, remember that biotin is not a bad vitamin or a bad guy at all.
[56:21] But it alters, skews, interferes with about 150 lab results. And so if you are drawing those labs
[56:32] and your patient is taking biotin in various forms, their labs are not going to be accurate.
[56:40] They're going to be skewed high. Some of them will be skewed low. So you want to make sure
[56:44] that they all know where to find the biotin. So most of the data says to stop biotin at least three
[56:54] days. That's not adequate if somebody's using like hair vitamins, because they'll have thousands,
[57:02] 10, 15, 30,000 micrograms of biotin in that product. And so it takes about one day for
[57:12] every hundred mics to clear the system. So I don't do that math with patients. I just tell them
[57:19] stop it about a week ahead of time, because that's generally enough for most of the biotin
[57:24] to clear their system. If you want the full list and exactly how it's skewed, you can google
[57:32] University of Iowa biotin and they have a whole PDF document of all of the skewed results,
[57:40] whether it's high or low, and you can have that. I just look it up if I need it.
[57:47] The four base ingredients that patients have to have for optimizing their thyroid
[57:52] are going to be iodine, vitamin D, iron, and protein. And so those are all products we want
[57:58] to recommend safely and carefully for patients. You want to be very careful recommending thyroid
[58:06] supplement combos, because many of them include selenium, iron, and chromium. All of those are
[58:12] heavy metals and unsafe for long-term use. Remember that six ounce, I don't remember
[58:19] the numbers exactly, but a six ounce red meat steak, like a ribeye or sirloin,
[58:26] is going to have almost 200% of the needed selenium. Why would a patient need to take selenium
[58:33] in any supplement form if they're eating red meat four or five days a week, which is bare minimum
[58:38] what I recommend. It's the most nutrient dense food out there, and so that food provides most
[58:47] of these ingredients. And so if you recommend thyroid combo supplements, keep in mind they're
[58:53] going to raise T4, they're not going to raise T3. T3 is what we want to raise. T4 is not
[58:59] converting properly for most of the people we are taking care of. And so raising T4 is not going to
[59:05] help them. You want to make sure they're getting iodine either in their salt or their supplement.
[59:10] I typically recommend no more than 300 micrograms of iodine. I typically use the pure
[59:17] encapsulations iodine product. I think it's 225 or 250 mics. In that, if a patient gets
[59:25] two teaspoons of iodized salt every day, they do get about 150, 160 micrograms of iodine,
[59:33] which is the RDA. So if they're using iodized salt, the label must say iodized for it to count.
[59:41] They can get two teaspoons of salt, and that iodized salt will be sufficient. They won't
[59:46] need a supplement. The only exception for iodine supplements is if patients have BPH
[59:54] or fibrocystic breast disease. Men that have BPH are found to have low iodine. Recent studies
[60:03] are showing this in the past year and a half or so. Same with fibrocystic breast disease in our
[60:10] women. And so I have been putting those two classifications of patients on up to 3,000
[60:18] micrograms of iodine a day to see if it helps with their fibrocystic breast disease
[60:26] and or their BPH. Ashwagandha is the only supplement that I have found that helps raise
[60:34] free T3 at all, and it only raises it a tiny bit. So if you have somebody, for instance,
[60:40] that comes to you and is mildly sub clinically hypothyroid, but they want something to boost
[60:46] them up. Let's say they're at 2.9 or 3.0 for a free T3 level. They probably don't need
[60:52] medicine. They probably need iodine. They need the ingredients to make a thyroid hormone,
[60:58] but you could add their iodine and their B and their iron and their protein and all the stuff,
[61:02] and then tell them to use some ashwagandha 700 or 800 milligrams once or twice a day,
[61:08] and that might help get them to like 3.2 or 3.3. It's not going to massively raise their
[61:15] free T3 all the way up to the top of the range, but ashwagandha is a great supplement. It's an
[61:22] adaptogen, so it helps with stress. It helps with rest, relaxation. I typically dose it at bedtime
[61:28] unless they need it in the daytime. This is my preferred iron and vitamin D. I like the
[61:36] Ferrosol and the Ostio PX products because they really do work toward improving iron values.
[61:44] I typically dose the Ferrosol twice a day as a prescription, not as a supplement,
[61:51] and so make sure your patients understand that you want them taking this twice a day
[61:55] and that it needs to be taken with a lot of vitamin C, not for the iron sake. Remember
[62:01] that all that extra vitamin C is being used to fix other things, but we connect it to the iron
[62:09] because we know vitamin C does aid in iron absorption and use. Vitamin D is crucial to
[62:15] get all the ingredients that are in Ostio PX at the same time, so this puts it all in one peel,
[62:22] and then they have to take this peel in between bites of fatty food,
[62:25] preferably lunch or dinner, not breakfast.
[62:28] Teaching patients how to take iron can be a real challenge,
[62:37] so if you want to screenshot this, I made this for patients and providers, but this is a great
[62:46] graphic to use if you want to save it and edit it out and get down to just the great part you can.
[62:53] Remember that if the ferritin is lower than 45-ish, the cardiac muscle is a little bit at risk because
[63:04] of the lack of oxygenation going on, and so you'll wind up with dysrhythmias like AFib
[63:11] and various other palpitations and crazy wacky rhythms, so get them on some iron
[63:20] twice a day and then get them to taking their D in between bites of fatty food.
[63:26] I know eliminating grains is challenging. I understand that, but the only way to truly
[63:31] improve health is to get rid of them, so really work with your patients to teach them and help
[63:37] them understand how important it is because of how it impacts their iron absorption.
[63:46] Emethyst, thanks for posting the PDF on the bio-immune assay tables from
[63:58] online. Kelly, I'm having a tough time with this lately. Yes, everyone does.
[64:03] When patients are struggling with getting rid of grains, I always recommend the books Brain
[64:08] Brain and Wheat Belly. They are the two OGs. They are the best books for patients to read
[64:15] and to really grasp how inflammatory grains are. The first thing that happens is grief.
[64:27] What happens when you lose something? You go through the five stages of grief, right?
[64:33] People don't want to give up their grains, and so they think,
[64:39] oh, well, I'll do gluten-free, and so that'll help a little, but not enough, and then they'll say,
[64:46] well, then I'll get rid of this thing, and then I'll get like they work their way through,
[64:50] and that's okay if they're having to do it step by step. That's better than not at all,
[64:55] but what happens is important for us to help explain to patients. They need to understand
[65:03] that grains break down to phytates, and phytates block iron absorption.
[65:08] Why are all these people low ferritin? They don't eat red meat, and they eat tons of grains. Those
[65:13] two things by themselves drive low ferritin. On top of that, most of our patients are women,
[65:20] and we lose blood every month until we're menopausal, and so the blood loss is constant
[65:27] on a monthly cycle, and then the daily intake is challenging for the body to get adequate nutrients
[65:35] from, and so it's important that we just keep teaching and teaching and teaching until they
[65:41] start understanding and getting better with it. B12 is essential. I like methyl or hydroxy or
[65:56] adenosyl forms of B12. Never cyan cobalamin. Cyan cobalamin cannot be absorbed well by most
[66:04] of us, at least half of us, but B12 by itself is pretty useless. You always want to use a B complex
[66:14] in not just the B12 ingredient. If you're talking about anti-inflammatories, these are some of my
[66:22] favorites. I like high dose vitamin C and vitamin D. If you are sick, if you have a sore throat,
[66:29] you can both boost these doses up really high. There is such a thing called a vitamin D hammer.
[66:36] We typically will use that when we start getting sickly, start having a runny nose,
[66:42] cough, sore throats will boost up to about 20,000 units of vitamin D every day. I do not do that
[66:51] with the osteopics. You cannot quadruple or, I don't know what eight times would be, but
[66:58] you can't do that with all those other ingredients. We do keep a plain vitamin D that doesn't have
[67:05] anything else in it. I think it's a neurobiologics vitamin D product from Fullscript. When we get
[67:13] sick, we add that vitamin D to our osteopics so that we boost our vitamin D intake for
[67:22] usually five to 10 days is all it takes for the immune system to respond to those colds and flu
[67:29] bugs. Sometimes we'll add the chlorella. If the gout flares, if John's gout's slaring up,
[67:36] we'll add chlorella through the day. We'll add the shankopedra. Resveratrol is being used currently
[67:45] by lots of your urogynecology folks for various female and male urological issues. Resveratrol is
[67:56] great for anti-inflammatory purposes. Cocutin and fish oil are also really good anti-inflammatories.
[68:05] Black seed oil and black cumin seed are great antioxidants and anti-inflammatories, but most
[68:13] of these shouldn't be taken long, long term. You take them for two to four weeks while you're sick
[68:21] or exposed and getting well, but you don't use them all the time. It's kind of like,
[68:26] would you put somebody on a moxacillin for two years? No. Why? Because it's not meant to be
[68:35] taken that way. It's a short term, acute illness kind of medicine, and that's what most of these
[68:42] anti-inflammatory agents are for. Keep that in mind.
[68:48] Oil of oregano is one of my favorite candida recommendations. People think they have a
[68:59] yeast infection, or if they know they have a yeast infection, they have the rash under their skin
[69:05] folds, vaginal yeast infections. Oil of oregano is great to take care of that. Some people will
[69:14] even give it to their dogs when they have a yeasty kind of skin stuff, mangy stuff going on.
[69:22] You can dose these with a couple of different doses here.
[69:31] This is the vitamin D that we do use when we get sick. We just bump it up. We don't bump anything
[69:38] else. We've tried. My husband has gout because of his diuretic use. I've been working recently
[69:52] on tweaking some things, so we have changed some things, and he's not using as many gout
[69:56] supplements as he was. He used to take Lasix 23 tablets by two or three o'clock in the afternoon.
[70:06] He would take one tablet, two tablets early morning, and one tablet around noonish, one o'clock.
[70:16] The first things we used were the ones on the left on the purple graphic. We used those for a while.
[70:24] Things got better, but then I kept reading and learning and studying,
[70:27] and then we started using the ones on the orange graphic.
[70:31] The ones on the orange graphic are the ones we currently use for his gout. However, we no
[70:39] longer use these on a daily basis because I've been tweaking some things. What I did is I got to dig
[70:48] in into Lasix and sporonylactone. He has congestive heart failure, so those are the two typical
[70:54] recommendations for CHF edema. I got to dig in and realize that the Lasix is more likely to cause
[71:03] gout flares than the sporonylactone, so I took him off the Lasix and put him on sporonylactone
[71:10] twice a day instead of once a day. Now he's getting sporonylactone early morning and then another
[71:17] dose around lunchtime or one or two o'clock in the afternoon, usually not after two
[71:21] because you know diuretics will work on you overnight. So now if he thinks he's having a
[71:28] gout flare, it still will happen on occasion because he is not a compliant eater by any stretch
[71:35] of the imagination, and so sometimes it's the things he eats or drinks that will make his
[71:40] gout flare up. So now when it starts to flare, we add the chancaperta and the chlorella right away
[71:48] and sometimes the AI formula. The AI formula contains the quercetin, tumeric, bromelain, ginger,
[71:57] and so those really help cut back on gout pain and flares.
[72:05] So what else have I been tweaking for his gout? Well, the other thing we like for his gout is
[72:11] a peptide. We've been using MOTS C, M-O-T-S-C, and so as long as he's on the MOTS C, we typically don't
[72:24] see much of a gout flare. We have been out for the last couple weeks and last week he did have
[72:29] a little bit of a flare, but it was actually way less troublesome, way less painful than the
[72:35] gout flares he had had before we changed diuretics around. Inositol is one of my favorite supplements

[93:21] to work in one whole system. The body is one whole system. That's why mainstream medicine
[93:27] struggles because they parcel and sort it all out. You go to one endocrinologist for diabetes and
[93:33] a different one for your thyroid. You go to a different GYN provider for your reproductive
[93:38] hormones because neither diabetes nor endo for thyroid want to touch their hormones. Like
[93:46] everybody wants their own territory and they don't want you overlapping or stepping into that.
[93:51] But that's a problem because it all overlaps in the body. The chemistry is all interacting
[93:57] together. All of the intake drives ATP production. So it really is crucial that patients understand
